react_questions.question: What is the Virtual DOM and how does React use it for optimization?
react_questions.answer: The Virtual DOM is an in-memory representation of the actual DOM. React creates a Virtual DOM tree after each state change. React compares (diffs) the new Virtual DOM with the previous one, identifying changed elements. Only changed elements update in the actual DOM. This reduces expensive DOM manipulations, improving performance significantly.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of reconciliation in React.
react_questions.answer: Reconciliation is the algorithm React uses to compare Virtual DOM versions and update the actual DOM. Process: React renders new Virtual DOM, compares with previous version (diffing), identifies differences, updates only changed elements. Key principle: React uses element keys to identify list items across renders. Reconciliation minimizes DOM mutations.

react_questions.question: What is the difference between functional components and class components?
react_questions.answer: Functional components are functions returning JSX. Class components extend React.Component with render() method. Functional components: simpler, smaller bundle size, easier to test, use hooks for state/lifecycle. Class components: lifecycle methods, complex state management, more verbose. Modern React favors functional components with hooks.

react_questions.question: Explain the useState hook and its purpose.
react_questions.answer: useState adds state to functional components. Returns array: [state, setState]. Syntax: const [count, setCount] = useState(0). setState updates state and triggers re-render. Multiple useState calls create independent state variables. Initial value can be primitive or function (lazy initialization). Replaces this.state in class components.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of the useEffect hook?
react_questions.answer: useEffect runs side effects in functional components. Syntax: useEffect(callback, dependencies). Runs after render by default. Dependencies array controls when it runs: no array (every render), empty array (once on mount/unmount), [deps] (when dependencies change). Returns cleanup function for cleanup on unmount/before re-run. Replaces componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmount.

react_questions.question: What is the importance of the dependency array in useEffect?
react_questions.answer: Dependency array controls useEffect execution frequency. No array: runs after every render (performance risk, infinite loops possible). Empty array: runs only on mount/unmount (perfect for initialization). [dep1, dep2]: runs when any dependency changes. Must include all external values used in effect. Missing dependencies cause bugs; unnecessary dependencies cause unnecessary runs.

react_questions.question: Explain the difference between useCallback and useMemo hooks.
react_questions.answer: useMemo memoizes computed values preventing recalculation. Syntax: const value = useMemo(() => expensiveCalculation(), [deps]). useCallback memoizes function references preventing recreation. Syntax: const callback = useCallback(() => {...}, [deps]). useMemo returns value, useCallback returns function. Both optimize performance by preventing child re-renders. Only use if performance issues exist.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useContext hook?
react_questions.answer: useContext accesses context value without Consumer component. Create context: const MyContext = React.createContext(). Provide value: <MyContext.Provider value={value}>. Access: const value = useContext(MyContext). Avoids prop drilling (passing through intermediate components). Re-renders only when context value changes.

react_questions.question: Explain the useRef hook and its use cases.
react_questions.answer: useRef creates mutable object persisting across re-renders. Syntax: const ref = useRef(initialValue). Returns .current property. Use cases: DOM manipulation (focus, play video), storing mutable values (timers, previous value). Changes don't trigger re-renders. Alternative to state when re-render not needed.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useReducer hook?
react_questions.answer: useReducer manages complex state with action-based updates. Syntax: const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState). dispatch sends actions to reducer function. Reducer returns new state. Better than useState for: complex state logic, state depending on previous state, multiple related state updates. Similar to Redux reducers.

react_questions.question: Explain prop drilling and solutions to avoid it.
react_questions.answer: Prop drilling is passing data through multiple intermediate components not using it. Problem: hard to maintain, prop changes require updating all intermediate components. Solutions: Context API (useContext), state management (Redux, Zustand, Recoil), component composition (children pattern), refactor component structure to reduce nesting depth.

react_questions.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled components?
react_questions.answer: Controlled components: React state manages form element value. Requires onChange handler updating state. Value always reflects state. More control, but more code. Example: <input value={state} onChange={e => setState(e.target.value)} />. Uncontrolled components: DOM manages state, accessed via refs. Simpler for simple forms. Harder to validate.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of lifting state up in React.
react_questions.answer: Lifting state up moves state from child components to common parent when multiple children need shared state. Parent manages state, passes as props to children. Enables state sharing and synchronization. Centralizes state management. Alternative: Context API or external state management for deep trees.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of React.memo?
react_questions.answer: React.memo memoizes functional components preventing unnecessary re-renders. Compares props (shallow comparison). Syntax: React.memo(Component). Only re-renders if props change. Second parameter accepts custom comparison function for deep comparison. Optimizes performance for expensive components. Only use if proven performance issue; adds overhead.

react_questions.question: Explain higher-order components (HOC) pattern.
react_questions.answer: HOC is function taking component and returning enhanced component. Syntax: const Enhanced = withEnhancement(MyComponent). Pattern for reusing component logic. Use cases: code reuse, state abstraction, props manipulation, authentication. Examples: withRouter, withTheme. Modern alternative: custom hooks. Still useful for wrapping third-party components.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of render props pattern?
react_questions.answer:
Render props is pattern passing function as prop. Component calls function to determine render output. Syntax:
{data}
} />. Enables code reuse, state sharing. Less popular than hooks but still useful. Combines multiple render props requires nested functions.

react_questions.question: Explain conditional rendering approaches in React.
react_questions.answer: Approaches: if-else statements, ternary operator (condition ? true : false), logical AND (condition && component), switch statements, component composition. Ternary and && most common. Avoid inline if-else in JSX. Component composition cleanest for complex logic. Extract conditionals to separate functions.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of keys in React lists?
react_questions.answer: Keys identify list items across renders. Help React track element identity. Syntax: <li key={id}>. Without keys, React uses index (problematic if list reorders). Use stable, unique IDs (not index). Missing keys cause bugs with: list state, form inputs, list animations. Critical for performance and correctness.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of error boundaries in React.
react_questions.answer: Error boundaries catch JavaScript errors in child components. Display fallback UI preventing app crashes. Implement: class component with componentDidCatch() lifecycle, or useErrorHandler() hook. Catch rendering errors only, not async/event handlers. Wrap top-level and route components.

react_questions.question: What is the difference between shallow and deep equality checks?
react_questions.answer: Shallow equality: compares only top-level properties. Objects/arrays are equal only if same reference. Deep equality: recursively compares nested properties. React.memo does shallow comparison by default. Provide custom comparison for deep checking. useMemo/useCallback dependencies use shallow comparison.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of code splitting in React.
react_questions.answer: Code splitting divides bundle into smaller chunks loaded on demand. React.lazy() loads components dynamically. Syntax: const LazyComponent = React.lazy(() => import('./Component')). Wrap with Suspense: <Suspense fallback={<Loading />}><LazyComponent /></Suspense>. Improves initial load time. Route-based code splitting most effective.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of Suspense in React?
react_questions.answer: Suspense handles asynchronous operations elegantly. Wraps async components/data fetching. Shows fallback UI while loading. Syntax: <Suspense fallback={<Loading />}><AsyncComponent /></Suspense>. Catches thrown promises from async operations. Simplifies async code, improves UX.

react_questions.question: Explain React.StrictMode and its purpose.
react_questions.answer: StrictMode highlights potential problems in development. Doesn't affect production. Checks: unsafe lifecycles, legacy string refs, unexpected side effects. Runs effects twice intentionally to detect side effects. Helps identify bugs early. Wrap app or specific components: <StrictMode><App /></StrictMode>.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useLayoutEffect hook?
react_questions.answer: useLayoutEffect is similar to useEffect but runs synchronously after DOM mutations before paint. Use cases: measuring DOM, adjusting layout. Can cause performance issues if not careful. Runs before browser paints, blocking render. useEffect usually preferred unless DOM measurement needed.

react_questions.question: Explain custom hooks and how to create them.
react_questions.answer: Custom hooks are functions starting with 'use' reusing stateful logic. Extract component logic into custom hook. Syntax: function useCustom() { const [state, setState] = useState(0); return { state, setState }; }. Combine multiple hooks. Enable code reuse across components. Follow rules of hooks.

react_questions.question: What are the rules of hooks in React?
react_questions.answer: Rule 1: Only call hooks at top level (not inside loops, conditions, functions). Rule 2: Only call hooks from functional components or custom hooks. Violating rules causes bugs with hook state. ESLint plugin enforces these rules. Critical for hook reliability.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of fragments in React.
react_questions.answer: Fragments allow returning multiple elements without wrapper div. Syntax: <></> or <React.Fragment>. Benefits: cleaner DOM, no extra wrapper divs breaking CSS Grid/Flexbox. Especially useful in maps returning multiple elements. Fragments have no props (except key in maps).

react_questions.question: What is the difference between state and props?
react_questions.answer: Props: read-only inputs from parent to child. State: local data managed by component. Props flow down, state is local. Changing props requires parent change. State changes trigger re-render. Props passed as function arguments, state uses useState hook. Props enable component reusability.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of default props.
react_questions.answer: Default props provide fallback values when props not provided. Class components: static defaultProps = {}. Functional components: Component.defaultProps = {}. Or use destructuring: function Component({ name = 'Guest' }) {}. Improve component robustness, reduce null checks.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of PropTypes?
react_questions.answer: PropTypes validates prop types at runtime. Development-only checks (removed in production). Syntax: MyComponent.propTypes = { name: PropTypes.string }. Catches prop type errors early. Improves debugging. Alternative: TypeScript for static typing. Common validations: string, number, bool, array, object, func.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of composition in React.
react_questions.answer: Composition builds components using other components. Children pattern: <Container><Child /></Container>. Enables flexible, reusable components. Prefer composition over inheritance. Components receive children as prop. Highly flexible architecture.

react_questions.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled forms?
react_questions.answer: Controlled forms: React state manages form data. State updates on every input change. Value prop reflects state. More control for validation, formatting. Uncontrolled forms: DOM manages data, accessed via ref. Simpler for simple forms. Harder to validate real-time.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of form submission in React.
react_questions.answer: Form submission: prevent default (e.preventDefault()), collect form data from state or refs, validate, submit. Prevent page reload. Use controlled components for validation. Handle submission with onClick or onSubmit handler. Show success/error messages.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useContext and Context API?
react_questions.answer: Context API provides way to pass data through component tree without prop drilling. Create: React.createContext(). Provide: Context.Provider with value. Consume: useContext(Context). Solves prop drilling. Can be used for: themes, authentication, language, global state.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of provider pattern in React.
react_questions.answer: Provider pattern wraps application/subtree providing context values. Create context, wrap with Provider, consume with useContext. Enables global state management. Multiple providers enable nested contexts. Context value changes trigger re-renders in consumers.

react_questions.question: What is the difference between local and global state?
react_questions.answer: Local state: component-specific data (useState). Used for form inputs, UI toggles. Global state: shared across many components (Context, Redux). Used for authentication, theme, user data. Balance between local and global state for optimal performance.

react_questions.question: Explain Redux and its core concepts.
react_questions.answer: Redux is state management library. Core: Store (state tree), Actions (describe changes), Reducers (process actions, return new state), Dispatch (trigger actions). Benefits: predictable state, time-travel debugging, DevTools. Downsides: boilerplate, learning curve. Alternatives: Zustand, Recoil, MobX.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of Redux middleware?
react_questions.answer: Middleware intercepts actions before reducers. Use cases: logging, API calls, async operations. Popular: redux-thunk (functions), redux-saga (generators). Middleware pattern: (store) => (next) => (action) => next(action). Enable side effects in Redux.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of selectors in Redux.
react_questions.answer: Selectors extract specific state pieces. Syntax: const selectTodos = state => state.todos. Benefits: single source of truth, refactor-safe, memoization. Reselect library for memoized selectors preventing unnecessary re-renders. Improve performance, maintainability.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of React Router?
react_questions.answer: React Router enables client-side routing in SPAs. Components: BrowserRouter, Route, Link, Navigate. Route maps URL paths to components. Link navigation without page reload. Enable multi-page experience in SPA. Nested routing for complex navigation.

react_questions.question: Explain route parameters and query strings.
react_questions.answer: Route parameters: URLs with dynamic values (/users/:id). Extract with useParams(). Query strings: URL search parameters (?sort=name). Extract with useSearchParams(). Route params for resource identification. Query strings for filtering, pagination, sorting.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of programmatic navigation in React Router?
react_questions.answer: Programmatic navigation changes routes via code. Use navigate() hook from useNavigate(). Syntax: navigate('/path') or navigate('/path', { state: {} }). Replace history: navigate('/path', { replace: true }). Use for conditional navigation, post-submission redirect.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of lazy loading routes.
react_questions.answer: Lazy loading routes loads route components on demand. Syntax: const Component = React.lazy(() => import('./Component')). Wrap with Suspense. Reduces initial bundle size. Each route chunk loads when navigated. Improves performance for apps with many routes.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of route protection?
react_questions.answer: Route protection restricts access to certain routes. Common use: private routes for authenticated users. Implement: custom ProtectedRoute component checking authentication. Redirect to login if not authenticated. Enable permission-based routing (admin routes). Improve security.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of form libraries in React.
react_questions.answer: Form libraries simplify complex form management. Examples: React Hook Form, Formik. Features: validation, error handling, field-level re-render optimization. React Hook Form: minimal re-renders, small bundle. Formik: comprehensive features, larger bundle. Choose based on complexity.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of validation in forms?
react_questions.answer: Validation ensures data correctness before submission. Types: client-side (instant feedback), server-side (security). Libraries: Yup, Zod, Joi. Validate: type, format, length, uniqueness. Show errors to users. Improve UX, security, data quality.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of form reset in React.
react_questions.answer: Form reset clears all form values to initial state. Methods: controlled forms reset state, uncontrolled forms use ref.current.reset(). Useful after form submission. Provide reset button for user experience. Clear form data, reset errors.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of data fetching in React?
react_questions.answer: Data fetching retrieves data from APIs. Use useEffect for side effects. Fetch data on mount (empty dependency array). Handle loading, error states. Use AbortController for cleanup. Libraries: axios, fetch API, React Query, SWR. Separate data fetching logic for reusability.

react_questions.question: Explain React Query and its benefits.
react_questions.answer: React Query simplifies server state management. Handles caching, synchronization, background updates. Features: automatic refetching, stale data, retry logic. Syntax: const { data, isLoading, error } = useQuery(['key'], fetchFn). Separates server state from UI state. Reduces boilerplate.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of SWR (stale-while-revalidate)?
react_questions.answer: SWR is data fetching library optimizing performance. Serves stale cache while fetching fresh data. Revalidates in background. Syntax: const { data, error } = useSWR('/api/data', fetcher). Auto-refetching, error handling, deduplication. Lightweight alternative to React Query.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of optimistic updates.
react_questions.answer: Optimistic updates update UI before server confirmation. User sees instant feedback. Rollback if server fails. Example: delete item immediately, remove from UI, revert if delete fails. Improves perceived performance, UX. Requires careful state management.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of pagination in React?
react_questions.answer: Pagination divides large datasets into pages. Track current page, items per page. Implement: page number input, previous/next buttons, page size selector. Improves performance, UX. Prevent loading huge datasets. Combine with sorting, filtering.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of infinite scroll in React.
react_questions.answer: Infinite scroll loads more items as user scrolls bottom. Detect scroll position (Intersection Observer). Fetch next batch on trigger. Append to existing list. Better UX than pagination for mobile. Implement with libraries: react-infinite-scroll-component. Better performance than loading all upfront.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of search and filter functionality?
react_questions.answer: Search filters data by text. Filter narrows results by criteria. Implement: input onChange state update, filter array, display results. Debounce search for performance (avoid excessive updates). Client-side: filter already-loaded data. Server-side: query API with search parameter.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of sorting in React.
react_questions.answer: Sorting arranges data by criteria (name, date, price). Ascending/descending toggle. Implement: sort button, state tracking sort field/order, array.sort(). Display indicator showing current sort. Combine with filtering, pagination. Server-side sorting for large datasets.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of animations in React?
react_questions.answer: Animations enhance UX, provide feedback. React-specific libraries: React Spring, Framer Motion. CSS alternatives: CSS animations, transitions. Animate: component enter/exit, state changes, interactions. Don't overuse (performance, distraction). Improve perceived performance.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of CSS-in-JS in React.
react_questions.answer: CSS-in-JS writes styles in JavaScript. Libraries: Styled Components, Emotion. Benefits: scoped styles, dynamic styling, JavaScript logic. Downsides: bundle size, runtime performance. Popular in React ecosystem. Alternative: CSS Modules.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of Styled Components?
react_questions.answer: Styled Components enables writing scoped styles in JavaScript. Syntax: const Button = styled.button`...CSS...`. Supports dynamic styles via props. Auto-generates unique class names preventing conflicts. Supports nested selectors, media queries, global styles.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of component composition patterns.
react_questions.answer: Patterns: Container/Presentational (smart/dumb), render props, HOC, hooks. Container handles logic, presentational handles UI. Modern: custom hooks for logic reuse. Composition enables flexible, testable components. Mix patterns based on use case.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useImperativeHandle hook?
react_questions.answer: useImperativeHandle customizes instance value exposed via ref. Syntax: useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({...}), [deps]). Dangerous: violates component encapsulation. Use rarely (media control, focus management). Avoid for data passing; use props/state instead.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of forwardRef in React.
react_questions.answer: forwardRef forwards refs through functional components. Syntax: const Component = forwardRef((props, ref) => {...}). Functional components don't have instance (no implicit ref). forwardRef enables passing ref to child element. Use carefully; prefer callback refs.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useDebugValue hook?
react_questions.answer: useDebugValue displays custom labels in React DevTools. Syntax: useDebugValue(value). Useful in custom hooks for debugging. Only runs in DevTools inspection. Improve development experience for library developers.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of portals in React.
react_questions.answer: Portals render components outside parent DOM hierarchy. Syntax: ReactDOM.createPortal(component, targetNode). Use cases: modals, tooltips, dropdowns escaping overflow. Maintains React component tree. Event propagation still works normally.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of React DevTools?
react_questions.answer: React DevTools browser extension for debugging. Features: component tree inspection, state/props viewing, hook values, profiling, time-travel debugging. Enable: render reason tracking, highlight updates. Essential for development.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of performance profiling in React.
react_questions.answer: Profiler measures render times, re-render reasons. Use React DevTools Profiler tab. Identify slow components. Measure before/after optimization. Look for: unexpected re-renders, unnecessary renders, excessive computation. Guide optimization efforts.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of preventing unnecessary re-renders?
react_questions.answer: Unnecessary re-renders waste resources, slow apps. Techniques: React.memo, useMemo, useCallback, keys in lists, state structure. Identify unnecessary renders with DevTools. Profile before optimizing (avoid premature optimization).

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of state structure optimization.
react_questions.answer: Optimal state structure: flat (avoid deep nesting), normalized (no duplication), grouped by feature or section. Avoid storing derived data (calculate on demand). Minimize what's in state. Good structure enables efficient updates, easy debugging.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of useTransition hook?
react_questions.answer: useTransition marks updates as non-urgent. Syntax: const [isPending, startTransition] = useTransition(). Wrap state updates in startTransition. Keeps UI responsive during heavy computations. Show pending state. Enable smooth UX with heavy updates.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of useDeferredValue hook.
react_questions.answer: useDeferredValue defers value updates. Syntax: const deferredValue = useDeferredValue(value). Useful for expensive updates (search results). Show stale UI while updating. Smooth UX. Keep input responsive while processing.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of testing React components?
react_questions.answer: Testing ensures component reliability. Types: unit (individual components), integration (multiple components), E2E (user workflows). Tools: Jest, React Testing Library, Enzyme. Best practices: test behavior, not implementation. Cover edge cases, error states.

react_questions.question: Explain React Testing Library and testing philosophy.
react_questions.answer: React Testing Library encourages testing from user perspective. Query elements as users do (role, text, label). Avoid testing implementation details. Philosophy: tests resemble real usage. Promotes accessible components, maintainable tests. Preferred over Enzyme.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of mocking in React testing?
react_questions.answer: Mocking replaces real modules/APIs with test doubles. Mock functions: jest.fn(). Mock modules: jest.mock(). Mock APIs: MSW (Mock Service Worker). Isolate component testing, control external behavior. Enable testing error scenarios, specific responses.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of snapshot testing.
react_questions.answer: Snapshot testing captures rendered output, compares against baseline. Syntax: expect(component).toMatchSnapshot(). First run creates baseline. Reruns compare against baseline. Use with caution (catch unintended changes). Combine with behavioral tests.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of async testing in React?
react_questions.answer: Async testing handles async operations (API calls, timeouts). Use waitFor() for async updates. Syntax: await waitFor(() => expect(...).toBeInTheDocument()). Test loading states, error handling. Enable realistic testing of async behavior.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of accessibility in React.
react_questions.answer: Accessibility ensures usability for disabled users. Implement: semantic HTML, ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, focus management. Test with screen readers. Libraries: react-a11y. Improves UX for all users. Legal requirement (WCAG guidelines).

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of ARIA labels in React?
react_questions.answer: ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes enhance accessibility. aria-label provides accessible name. aria-role defines element purpose. aria-expanded indicates state. aria-hidden hides from screen readers. Supplement semantic HTML. Screen readers announce ARIA attributes.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of keyboard navigation in React.
react_questions.answer: Keyboard navigation enables using apps without mouse. Implement: tabIndex for tab order, onKeyDown handlers for shortcuts. Focus management: focus() method, useRef. Show focus indicators. Test with Tab, Enter, Escape keys. Essential for accessibility.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of internationalization (i18n)?
react_questions.answer: i18n enables multiple language support. Libraries: react-i18next, react-intl. Translate strings, format dates/numbers/currency. Store translations separately. Enable language switching. Improve app accessibility to global users.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of theming in React.
react_questions.answer: Theming enables switching visual styles (dark/light mode). Approaches: CSS variables, Context API, styled-components. Provide theme object with colors, fonts, sizes. Consumers access via Context. Enable dynamic theming, user preferences.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of layout systems in React?
react_questions.answer: Layout systems organize components spatially. Use: CSS Grid, Flexbox. Component-based layouts: Layout wrapper components. Responsive layouts: media queries, breakpoints. Grid systems: Bootstrap, Material-UI. Enable flexible, maintainable layouts.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of component libraries.
react_questions.answer: Component libraries provide pre-built components. Examples: Material-UI, React Bootstrap, Chakra UI. Benefits: consistency, accessibility, time-saving. Downsides: customization limits, bundle size. Use for rapid development, startups. Build custom for unique designs.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of design systems?
react_questions.answer: Design systems document design patterns, components, guidelines. Enable consistency across products. Include: components, colors, typography, spacing. Tools: Storybook, Zeroheight. Improve scalability, team communication, accessibility.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of Storybook in React.
react_questions.answer: Storybook is tool for developing components in isolation. Write stories for different states. Benefits: documentation, testing, collaboration. Syntax: Story export with args. Addons for accessibility testing, visual testing. Improve component development workflow.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of Next.js framework?
react_questions.answer: Next.js is React framework for production. Features: file-based routing, SSR/SSG, API routes, image optimization. Improves performance, SEO, developer experience. Built-in optimizations. Ideal for full-stack applications.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of server-side rendering (SSR).
react_questions.answer: SSR renders React components on server, sends HTML to client. Benefits: faster initial load, better SEO, improved accessibility. Complexity: state hydration, async data fetching. Frameworks: Next.js, Remix. Trade-off: server load vs client performance.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of static site generation (SSG)?
react_questions.answer: SSG pre-builds pages at build time, serves static HTML. Benefits: fastest load, better SEO, scalability. Trade-off: rebuild needed for updates. Ideal for: blogs, documentation, marketing sites. Frameworks: Next.js, Gatsby.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of incremental static regeneration (ISR).
react_questions.answer: ISR revalidates static pages on-demand after specified time. Syntax: revalidate: 60 (seconds). Updates pages without full rebuild. Combines SSG benefits with fresh content. Perfect for semi-static content (blogs with frequent updates).

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of API routes in Next.js?
react_questions.answer: API routes create backend endpoints without separate server. Files: pages/api/route.js. Handle HTTP methods: GET, POST, etc. Integrate with database, external APIs. Deploy as serverless functions. Simplify full-stack development.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of middleware in Next.js.
react_questions.answer: Middleware runs before route handlers. Use cases: authentication, logging, redirects. Edge Middleware (edge runtime): ultra-low latency. Enable advanced routing, request transformation.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of image optimization?
react_questions.answer: Image optimization improves performance. Techniques: lazy loading, responsive sizes, compression, modern formats. Next.js Image component auto-optimizes. Benefits: faster load, reduced bandwidth. Critical for performance.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of bundle analysis.
react_questions.answer: Bundle analysis identifies large dependencies. Tools: webpack-bundle-analyzer, source-map-explorer. Identify bloat, optimize imports. Dynamic imports reduce bundle. Tree shaking removes unused code. Monitor bundle size in CI/CD.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of lazy loading components?
react_questions.answer: Lazy loading defers component loading until needed. React.lazy() + Suspense for route-based. Show loading UI. Reduces initial bundle. Improves first contentful paint. Especially useful for heavy components, many routes.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of memoization patterns.
react_questions.answer: Memoization caches computation results. React patterns: React.memo (components), useMemo (values), useCallback (functions). Prevents unnecessary recalculation, re-renders. Only use with proven performance gains. Measure impact.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of environment variables in React?
react_questions.answer: Environment variables store configuration. Different values per environment (dev, test, prod). React: REACT_APP_ prefix (client-side). Next.js: no prefix needed. Don't expose secrets (use backend). Customize builds per environment.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of error handling in React.
react_questions.answer: Error handling catches and manages errors gracefully. Approaches: error boundaries, try-catch, error callbacks. Show user-friendly messages. Log errors for debugging. Prevent app crashes. Enable recovery.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of loading states?
react_questions.answer: Loading states indicate ongoing operations. Show spinners, skeletons, disabled buttons. Prevent double-submission. Improve UX, feedback. Combine with error states for complete handling. Example: loading, success, error states.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of concurrent rendering.
react_questions.answer: Concurrent rendering interrupts and resumes rendering. Enables React to pause expensive renders. Keeps UI responsive to urgent updates. Features: useTransition, useDeferredValue. Modern React feature for performance.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of React 18 features?
react_questions.answer: React 18 introduces: automatic batching, transitions, Suspense. Improves performance, UX. automatic batching reduces renders. useTransition marks non-urgent updates. Suspense enables async component loading. Enable Concurrent Features gradually.

react_questions.question: Explain the concept of dependency management.
react_questions.answer: Manage React dependencies carefully. Regular updates for security, features. Use npm/yarn lock files for consistency. Audit vulnerabilities. Evaluate breaking changes. Use semantic versioning. Keep core and ecosystem packages updated.

react_questions.question: What is the purpose of performance budgets?
react_questions.answer: Performance budgets set limits on metrics: bundle size, load time, Lighthouse score. Enforce in CI/CD. Prevent regressions. Guide optimization efforts. Monitor against budget. Critical for maintaining performance as apps scale.
